summary: The notebook demonstrates a physics-informed 1D spectral modeling approach for the ARIEL data challenge. It processes raw detector frames through a multi-step calibration pipeline (ADC, dark current, linearity, CDS, flat-fielding) and spatial binning to extract 1D light curves. Instead of training a neural network, it analytically fits a polynomial with a transit shift parameter to identify ingress/egress breakpoints and derives a single scalar feature as the prediction. The author shows how domain knowledge and signal processing can yield a fast, interpretable baseline without heavy ML, achieving a competitive GLL score with minimal compute.
competition_flow: Reads raw detector parquet files, applies a multi-step physics-based calibration and spatial binning pipeline to extract 1D spectra, analytically fits transit breakpoints to derive a shift feature, and formats a submission with constant uncertainty.
data_reading: Reads detector signal and calibration frames from parquet files using pyarrow
Loads star metadata and wavelength arrays from CSVs
Loads preprocessed 1D spectra from numpy arrays
data_processing: Masks hot/dead pixels via sigma clipping on dark frames
Performs polynomial linearity correction using Horner's method
Subtracts dark current scaled by integration time
Applies Correlated Double Sampling (alternating frame differencing)
Bins temporal observations (15x for AIRS-CH0, 180x for FGS1)
Interpolates NaNs via convolution-based weight normalization
Averages spatial dimensions to collapse to 1D
Applies Savitzky-Golay smoothing and wavelength cropping
features_engineering: Detects transit ingress/egress breakpoints using the first derivative of smoothed signals
Fits a piecewise polynomial with a multiplicative shift parameter across pre-transit, transit, and post-transit regions
Extracts 1 - y_shift as the primary prediction feature
insights: Physics-based calibration pipelines can be efficiently implemented with vectorized tensor operations and multiprocessing.
Analytical signal processing and curve fitting can replace complex ML models for fast, interpretable baselines.
Assigning a constant uncertainty across all wavelengths is sufficient to achieve a strong initial Gaussian Log Likelihood score.
critical_findings: Hot and dead pixels are dynamically masked using sigma clipping on dark frames rather than relying solely on provided dead pixel maps.
The detector uses Correlated Double Sampling, requiring alternating frame differencing to extract the true signal.
NaN interpolation is handled via a custom convolution-based weight normalization to preserve spatial structure during gap filling.
